explanations.a: Correct. Capsaicin is classified as a protoalkaloid due to the presence of nitrogen in its structure.
explanations.b: Incorrect. Capsaicin is not a terpenoid.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Capsaicin is not a flavonoid.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Capsaicin is considered a protoalkaloid.

explanations.a: Incorrect. Sulphur is not the characteristic heteroatom of alkaloids.
explanations.b: Correct. Alkaloids are nitrogen-containing organic compounds.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Oxygen may be present but is not the defining heteroatom.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Phosphorus is not characteristic of alkaloids.

explanations.b: Incorrect. Bell pepper belongs to the Capsicum genus, although capsaicin content is negligible.
explanations.d: Correct. Kali mirch (black pepper) contains piperine, not capsaicin.

explanations.a: Correct. Capsaicin is primarily produced in the placental tissue associated with the ovary of chilli fruits.
explanations.b: Incorrect. Seeds do not synthesize capsaicin; they may only absorb some from surrounding tissues.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Sepals do not contain significant capsaicin.

explanations.a: Incorrect. This is higher than the reported melting point.
explanations.b: Incorrect. This is much higher than the reported melting point.
explanations.c: Correct. Pure capsaicin has a melting point around 62–65°C.
explanations.d: Incorrect. This is higher than the reported melting point.
